speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one of the things I neglected to mention, when-- as we went-- that we were led through the city, through that crazy mob that beat us up, we couldn't help but noticing many of the windows of the Jewish establishments and department stores being banged in and broken. And-- and there were a couple of banking houses that-- that were very prominent, by the name of [? Fidel ?] [? Humburger, ?] in-- in Karlsruhe. And they just through every imaginable piece of business machine out the window into the street. And it was a-- it was a day to remember. But in any case, so now, the engineer, Guggenheim family, they liquidated their business. And I had some distant relatives that lived in-- in Karlsruhe. I moved in with them. And it was impossible to-- to get a job or do anything. So the only place you could work, as allowed by the city of Karlsruhe, was there a-- an area outside where they-- there was a big trash dump, and to do-- to straighten out the area, to reclaim land. And I worked there with a bunch of other-- oh, maybe were about 25 other young Jewish men-- until the 26th of May, 1939. A few days earlier, I received a-- some advice by the-- the local government, that I have to leave Germany and rejoin my family in Poland. So on the 26th of May, 1939, I went on the train and got reunited with my family, including my father, who since already was in Poland, but came to-- to be with the rest of the family. And I was-- and arrived there a day later. And I was reunited with them on the 27th of May, 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what city?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Zbaszyn on the border where, still, many of the refugees already had left and were able to get resettled, either in Poland, or outside Poland, or whatever-- the cities. Some, in fact, even had permission to go back to Germany, collect their property, and then migrated to some other parts of the world. And because of our limited means, our family couldn't. But finally-- I would say, it must have been about one or two months later, it must-- no, it must have been in July of that year, my brother and I, we were able to enter an Ord school, that famous Ord school, even way back then. And we were able to-- they took us to Poznan, a city in Pomerania, which, at one time, even in fact, before World War I, belonged to Germany. But then, it was-- became part of Poland. And so we were then in Poznan. And we learned the metal trade by-- in-- in a-- in a-- in a factory, Jewish-owned tool factory, and with, perhaps, 25 or 30 other young Jewish men. And they put us up in some-- in-- in-- in-- in a home where we lived. And they fed us and so on. It was funded by the American Ord. And of course, right now, because of-- I remember then, I'm very much involved in the Ord organization and support them, the best I can. And so that was in-- in July. So it was July, August. One day, it was September 1, 1939, the boss from the factory, a Jewish man by the name of Ignac Tuch, he came and talked to us and said, a war has started, and the Germans attacked Poland. He asked us to go home. And on the way home-- that was mid-morning-- we heard the airplanes, and the bombs fell all around us. So that was the day the war began, World War II. And two days later, the Germans entered into Poznan, without a shot. And the whole war was over three weeks later, except Warsaw was the only city that held out. And that was the end. Then, the occupation began. But a month later, the-- an order came from the German authorities, because Poznan was part of Pomerania, which was the corridor, which was reclaimed by the Germans to be annexed to the Deutszche Reich. And the governor was Hans Frank. He gave an order, all Pomerania must be judenfrei.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they picked us up one morning to put us on trucks, and pushed us to Kalisz. And there, they put us in-- in-- in a-- in-- in-- in-- in the basement of-- of-- of the City Hall. We were stuck there for three days with no food, and nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they allowed us to leave, provided that we leave the city as quickly as possible. Somehow, we organized some horse and wagons from the Polish population and traveled from there to Lodz. And there are many, many cities. There's Opatowek, and there's Praszka, and there's Sieradz, Zdunska Wola, Pabianice, and finally Lodz. When we came to Sieradz-- no, when we came to-- to Zdunska Wola, which was a-- that day was one-- exactly one year after Kristallnacht. And I guess the Nazis wanted to celebrate. They rounded up-- there was a large Jewish population, that shtetled there in-- in-- in Zdunska Wola. And they rounded up everybody, maybe about 3,000 Jews, men. And we just happened to be there at the same time. And we were with some Jewish families. And that night, they picked everybody-- in the-- late in the evening, and they put us in the city square. And we were there all night. And it was dark, mind. It was-- was cold. It already was-- already was mid-November already. Well, it was November, 11th, to be exact. And finally, two days later, they took us from there into a prison, that famous Sieradz Penal Prison. And we were there for 11 days. It was brutal. They released us. And we finally got to-- to Lodz. And the Jewish community, they took us under their arms. And-- and we spent, oh, several weeks there. There was a-- a-- a-- a kibbutz, Kibbutz [? Barokow, ?] which was a form of woolen mill.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed there. But every day, the Nazis picked us up, and we worked at the railroad, in the Lodz railroad, and to remove the ice from the tracks, and so on, and so forth. It was a brutal job. Got beaten up almost every day. And a month later, they ordered everybody to move into the Lodz ghetto, which ultimately, on January 5, 1940, was closed. So I was with my brother. We-- we lived on [POLISH]. I remember the street we lived. We had a lot of friends, and so on. And there was no work. And we somehow existed. It was bad, but not brutal. It was somehow bearable at that time. Were your parents with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Parents were not with me. And I come back to that in a moment. In fact, I should [INAUDIBLE]. That was a good question. No. Two days prior to the attack on Poland-- my sister, incidentally, she already has-- she moved to-- from-- from Zbaszyn to Krakow, near Warsaw. She ended at a kibbutz, to ultimately go to Palestine, in those years. And my father and my mother was still left behind. Two days before the war broke out, we got a card from them that they're-- she's-- they're going to come through the station, Poznan. We should meet them at the-- at the train station. And we did. And that's the last time, that you will later on learn, that I saw my mother for six and a half years. But later on, I got reunited with my father. Anyway, so we were in this Lodz ghetto. And on the-- in early May, 6th, of 1940, I just didn't want to stay there. I asked my brother. He didn't want to leave. He'd befriended a young lady. He was a couple years older than I. He didn't want to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I left, and it wasn't easy. So I and a couple of other-- another young lady and another young gentleman-- we were-- the-- the Lodz ghetto, the fence in one area, on one side, the-- was bordering the Jewish cemetery. We stayed in the cemetery all night. And early in the morning, when-- after curfew, which 6:00 in the morning, we scaled the fence and escaped.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I somehow made my way to Warsaw. And there, I got reunited with my father. And by the way, Warsaw, at that time, had no ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find him?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew people. We knew somebody who knew somebody. I found him. I had an idea where he was. We had word. He lived with a family. But a few days later-- a few weeks later-- we noticed that they put fences, closing streets with brick walls. And that ultimately became the portions of the ghetto. And nobody could go beyond there. And then, finally, later on that year, was late in 1940 when they closed the Lodz ghetto. And then, things started to get tough. And-- and I have to tell you, you know, in-- in-- in rooms that would normally be, maybe, good for-- for two-- for two individuals, as many as six, eight people lived in that room. So we were assigned a room with a-- with another Jewish family. There were-- there were four of them, and two of us, so six people in one room. That was the bedroom, the living room, the kitchen, and you name it. But I was an enterprising individual, so I got myself-- I learned of some factory in the ghetto that was packaging spices, puddings, and baking powders, cinnamon, and pepper, and stuff like that. And I got a job with them. And I smuggled myself out every day, out of the ghetto, and sold that stuff on the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the Christian side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you had to-- had to pay somebody to smuggle yourself out. You had to pay somebody to smuggle yourself in. And of course, when you-- you take off your your-- your-- armband, which--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- what-- what person was that, the guy who took the money from you? Was it a Jew? Was it a German? Who was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jews and Pollacks. All depends where the business going. But it was no major-- that was just a little fee. But I did good business, and I supported my father that way. It was risky. And in fact, a few times, I almost got myself in real deep, deep trouble, but I somehow got out of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now, things started getting tougher. And you really-- eventually, you couldn't get out. They really closed every loophole, every exit, that-- except, you could get out if you went out with a work detail. So on a daily basis, the Nazis, the SS, and then-- and then the soldiers, they came inside to get-- and recruited Jewish men, and sometimes women, to work outside the ghetto. Approximately, when is that? What date?

speaker: interviewee
text: That would be in December, 1940, and early January, February, 1941. But I stayed there. One day, I heard of about-- of a camp outside Warsaw where they treat people fairly well. And I volunteered, when I heard where you have to go to go to that camp, which was a former-- oh, estate of a Polish count outside Warsaw, near-- near [INAUDIBLE] [? Ruciane, ?] where the-- where the-- where the-- the-- the Warsaw airport was. And I got myself a job there. You worked on the farm, or you took care of horses. And they had a lot of riding horses there. In fact, one day-- and I was assigned a job, take care of horses. And the German elite-- the officers-- periodically, they came there for-- for hunting.

speaker: interviewer
text: One day, I-- and I'd bring the horse out. It was Amy Goerring, the wife of Hermann Goerring, who came there to entertain herself on a-- on this estate, and went on hunting. You know, they call it [? polowania. ?] You know that expression?

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and that was-- but I was 19 months there. And it was fairly good, until it became not so good. And they started beating me up. I had to clean the-- the shoes of-- of the-- the-- of the SS. And he beat me up real brutally. So one day--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why? There was no reason why. He just didn't like the way I cleaned his shoes, so he just needed a whipping boy. And I was convenient there, and-- and he beat me up. But-- but there was one officer there-- in fact, he was an-- I think he was an SS man too, but he sort of liked me. One day, I said, I-- I have to go back to the ghetto. I've got bad teeth and I've got a toothache. Got to go to the dentist. I'll come back. Said, you go on the truck. You go there, and-- and the-- the-- the truck always stops there at the Pawiak prison, the famous Pawiak prison. And so I went there, and then never returned. And when I got back, I found out that my father had perished, that he died. So that was quite bad. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to send things that-- you were able to-- to get-- send packages there, but probably never got to him. And I-- and he just perished. I think he starved to death, or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went back into the ghetto, but there was no future there either. A couple days later, I went-- again. I heard of another camp, which was on the other side, near Warszawa Praga, a sizable project where they were building a fuel depot for their eventual plans to attack-- attack Russia. You know, that was in 1941. That was in 1941.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were-- there were about 3,000 Jewish workers. And a few years ago, I-- coincidentally, I met a Jewish man here in LA that also worked there. But he was pretending he was a-- a-- a-- an Aryan, a Pole. He speaks perfect Polish. And he worked there too, but not as a Jew. They could go home every night, but we had to go in a camp. That camp was fair. Nothing was-- it was no resort, I guarantee you, but it was fair. And they gave you halfway decent food. In fact, you got paid. The first few months, they paid everybody. It was something very, very unusual. In the Polish zloty, they paid you. And you got paid every week, until one Friday they said here. And they-- they-- they say here. Here, it's your-- your slip, what you worked, but you can't get any pay. And things started to get not so good. Worse. I suddenly caught malaria. So again, and-- and I just couldn't help it. And I asked to be taken to the ghetto. There was a hospital. The ghetto was still operating. It wasn't totally liquidated yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the-- what was the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: That would have been, now, in late 1942. That camp-- I-- I got staying power. I was there about 18 months in that camp. Then I asked be taken to the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Remind me, exactly, what it was called.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Kavenshein. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Kavenshein. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Kavenshein, ?] very popular, very-- a lot of people know about it. In fact, I-- when I was in-- in-- in-- in Poland there a couple years back, I went back there to visit all the areas that I spent the war years--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's there now?

speaker: interviewee
text: It what? Well, it-- it-- the-- where we lived, that was a-- a-- a brick factory, where they-- they-- brick and-- and-- and [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tiles.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. For the-- for the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Roof tiles.

speaker: interviewee
text: Roof tiles, yeah. And that's [? Kavenshein. ?] And it was a gigantic project. But anyway, so I asked to be taken to the ghetto and checked into the hospital. And they gave me-- and it was just like a regular hospital, with the Jewish doctors. They gave you quinine, which is an anti-malaria drug. And finally-- then one morning, I heard that they're liquidating the ghetto. And the Umschlagplatz was not too far from the area where the hospital was. And I just put two and two together. And I knew where the quinine was located. So that night, I went to the medicine cabinet. You would wonder why it would be so open. It was open. I mean, you could just open the thing. And I took three big bottles of quinine, found some clothes, and left. And smuggled myself out of the-- left the hospital. Went back to the Pawiak prison where they pick up every morning people to go into [? Kavenshein. ?] And back I went to the original place. And somehow, I nursed myself to health, by means of this medicine that I had.

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally-- that was the total of the period that I mentioned before. And on the 18th of July, 1943, one morning, maybe 5:00, 5:30 in the morning, the SS came charging into the camp. [INAUDIBLE] At that time, we were about 2,500 workers-- men, women, children. Rounded everybody up, and we had to walk to Rembertow. That's near-- near a train station, maybe about five miles from the camp, and where they loaded us into the cattle cars and took us to Treblinka.

speaker: interviewee
text: On a straight-- on a normal run from Rembertow to Treblinka, it probably would be two hours, maximum three hours. I think two and a half days, they ran us around, and around, and around, all over. We finally got there. I think it would have been about the 21st of July. And then, we arrived in Treblinka in a sand pit, gigantic sand pit where the cattle cars came in. And-- but the same part of that transport was a group of Jews that worked on the Ostbahn, in the other side of Warsaw. They were part of that transport. I think, a total of maybe 4,000 people arrived at that pit that morning. And the train arrives. And you look about you. And on the rim of that pit, the SS, and the guards, and the Ukrainians, they're all there. And then, the Hauptsturmfuhrer, the commandant of the-- of the Treblinka camp, that-- you're now in Treblinka. Hier wie Treblinka luft. I means, what you smell here is how Treblinka smells-- all the-- the burned bodies, and so on, and so forth. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who said that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the-- the commandant of the camp-- van Eupen, Hauptsurmfuhrer van Eupen. Very famous character. Good. Now--
